coredata.dc:title: Fluorine- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots for enhanced photoresponsivity in silicon photodetectors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Zhou
Nan, Feng
Zhang, Zhilong
Feng, Weiyu
Zhou, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Silicon (Si) photodetectors are of great importance due to their many scientific and industrial applications, including optical interconnects, spectroscopy, optical communications, and semiconductor device processing. However, the responsivity of Si photodetectors drops sharply at shorter wavelengths due to their high light absorption coefficient and increased reflectivity in this range. In this report, a hybrid Si photodetector architecture is demonstrated by integrating an ultrathin fluorine- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon quantum dots (fnCQDs) layer using a facile fabrication technique. The results show that the hybrid device achieves a broadband photoresponse. The optimized incorporation of fnCQDs enhances the short-wavelength range responsivity between 300 nm and 550 nm, while simultaneously maintaining nearly identical fast rise times at the peak as the conventional bare Si device under 0 V working voltage. Moreover, the photoresponsivity and gain of the engineered optimal device are found to be approximately 0.012 A/W and 328, respectively, at a wavelength of 365 nm. Additionally, the maximum photocurrent-to-dark current ratio (@365 nm) of this hybrid device under a 0.01 V bias reaches approximately 6200, nearly six times greater than that of the standard reference device. The proposed approach and findings demonstrate the significant potential of fnCQDs for applications in hybrid photodetectors and related technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Si photodiode
Responsivity
Ultraviolet (UV) radiation
Wavelength conversion

BK20201476, BK20201067).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) TEM image, high-resolution TEM, and corresponding FFT pattern of fnCQDs. (d–e) XPS survey scan of the acquired fnCQDs. (e–g) The corresponding high-resolution C 1s, F 1s and N 1s XPS spectrum of fnCQDs.

(a) FTIR spectra of fnCQDs. (b) UV–Vis absorption spectra of fnCQDs, with the inset showing the fnCQDs solution under ambient light. (c) Tauc plot of fnCQDs in solid powder form. (d) Photoluminescence spectra of fnCQDs measured at various excitation wavelengths.

(a) Schematic illumination of the hybrid Si structure. (b) A standard device and a hybrid device under ambient and UV light (@365 nm) irradiation, respectively. (c) SEM image of the fnCQDs nanoparticles assembled on the surface of a Si photodetector. (d) EDS mappings of Si, C, O, N and F, respectively.

(a) Photoresponsivity as a function of incident wavelength, ranging from 300 nm to 1100 nm. (b) Corresponding enhancement factor of the hybrid devices, measured across six samples within the wavelength range of 670 nm–1100 nm. (c) Average enhancement factor of photoresponsivity for the developed hybrid devices, measured using six devices within the wavelength range of 300 nm–550 nm. (d) Schematic diagram illustrating energy transfer in fnCQDs within the Si photodetector.

(a) IP/ID curves of a hybrid Si photodetector (fnD4) and a reference Si photodetector at 365 nm. (b) Photocurrent characteristics under varying incident light intensities (V = 0 V). (c) Wavelength-dependent photocurrent properties (V = 0 V). (d) Normalized time-dependent photoresponse of the hybrid Si photodetector (fnD4). (e) Magnified views of the temporal photocurrent response over one period and at the rising edge.

head.ce:title: Fluorine- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots for enhanced photoresponsivity in silicon photodetectors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Mathematics and Physics, Huaiyin Institute of Technology, Huai׳an 223003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Mechanical & Material Engineering Huaiyin Institute of Technology, Huaian, 223003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Mechanical & Material Engineering Huaiyin Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Mechanical & Material Engineering Huaiyin Institute of Technology, Huai ׳an, 223003, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Mathematics and Physics, Huaiyin Institute of Technology, Huai׳an, 223003, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Silicon (Si) photodetectors are of great importance due to their many scientific and industrial applications, including optical interconnects, spectroscopy, optical communications, and semiconductor device processing. However, the responsivity of Si photodetectors drops sharply at shorter wavelengths due to their high light absorption coefficient and increased reflectivity in this range. In this report, a hybrid Si photodetector architecture is demonstrated by integrating an ultrathin fluorine- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon quantum dots (fnCQDs) layer using a facile fabrication technique. The results show that the hybrid device achieves a broadband photoresponse. The optimized incorporation of fnCQDs enhances the short-wavelength range responsivity between 300 nm and 550 nm, while simultaneously maintaining nearly identical fast rise times at the peak as the conventional bare Si device under 0 V working voltage. Moreover, the photoresponsivity and gain of the engineered optimal device are found to be approximately 0.012 A/W and 328, respectively, at a wavelength of 365 nm. Additionally, the maximum photocurrent-to-dark current ratio (@365 nm) of this hybrid device under a 0.01 V bias reaches approximately 6200, nearly six times greater than that of the standard reference device. The proposed approach and findings demonstrate the significant potential of fnCQDs for applications in hybrid photodetectors and related technologies.

A hybrid Si photodetector architecture by integrating with an ultrathin fluorine and nitrogen co-doping carbon quantum dots (fnCQDs) layer is demonstrated. The photoresponsivity and gain of the engineered optimal device are found to be approximately 0.012 A/W and 328, respectively, at a wavelength of 365 nm.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the introduction in the 1960s, Si photodetectors have become a fundamental technology in photodetection [1,2], with widespread applications in optical interconnects, spectroscopy, optical communications, and semiconductor device processing [3–5]. Over the years, the advent of nanotechnology has further advanced the miniaturization and integration of Si photodetectors, securing their vital role in modern optoelectronic devices [6]. However, Si photodetectors suffer from significantly decreased responsivity at shorter wavelengths due to their high light absorption coefficient and increased reflectivity in this range [7,8]. In this regard, there has been a vigorous effort to improve high sensitivity within the shorter wavelength range for Si-based photodetectors, i.e., heterostructure architecture (e.g., graphene/Si, SiQDs/Gr/Si, WS2/Si, carbon quantum dots(CQDs)/Si) [9–12], Si nanowires [13], nano-porous Si [14], ZnO/micro-Si [15], InP/ZnS quantum dots [16] and many others. Nevertheless, finding an appropriate device architecture to harvest a wide range of light for economic, eco-friendly, and high-performance broadband Si-based photodetectors remains a challenge.

CQDs considered as a new member of fluorescent carbon material are becoming a promising alternative to metal-based quantum dots because of their favorable capabilities such as low cost, nontoxicity, bio sound chemical stability, and remarkable optical properties [17–19]. These favorable capabilities of CQDs exhibited a great potential for the applications in biomarker and bioimaging, light-emitting diodes, analytical chemistry, imaging, photovoltaics, sensing and so forth [20–23]. In our previous work, we demonstrated that the photoresponsivity of the Si photodetector can be enhanced ∼20 % within the relative short wavelength range between 300 nm and 600 nm with additional fluorescent CQDs layer [24]. However, the enhancement of the photoresponsivity this hybrid Si photodetector in the short wavelength range is limited due to the CQDs of low fluorescence quantum yield in aqueous solution. Intriguingly, Recent reports have demonstrated that nitrogen (N) and/or fluorine (F) doping can introduce a small bandgap in the electronic structure of CQDs, resulting in the formation of additional lone pairs of electrons [25]. This modification significantly alters the properties of CQDs, including their absorption spectrum, transparency, and photoluminescence, thereby highlighting their potential for various previously mentioned applications [26,27]. Hence, it would be meaningful to investigate the possibility of the fluorine and nitrogen co-doping carbon quantum dots (fnCQDs) acting as an functional layer for desired enhanced short-wavelength range responsivity of Si photodetectors and unveil the corresponding optoelectronic properties.

Herein, we demonstrated a hybrid Si photodetector architecture incorporating a functional layer of fnCQDs nanoparticles by a facile and scalable one-step drop-casting method. The as-synthesized water-soluble fnCQDs, characterized by high fluorescence quantum yield (absolute PLQY is about 56 %), were synthesized via a single-step hydrothermal process using a mixture of 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid and glycine, and subsequently deposited on the surface of a Si positive-intrinsic-negative (PIN) photodetector. Systematic experimental investigations demonstrate that the fabricated hybrid device exhibits a broadband photoresponse, primarily attributed to the unique optoelectronic properties of the fnCQDs layer. This layer efficiently converts incident short-wavelength light into long-wavelength light, which aligns with the high-response region of the PIN Si photodetector. Consequently, the converted light is more effectively captured, significantly enhancing the photoresponse in the short-wavelength range. The optimized integration of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (fnCQDs) significantly enhances the responsivity of the device in the short-wavelength range (300–550 nm) while preserving the fast rise times at the peak responsivity, comparable to those of the conventional bare silicon device under a zero-voltage operating condition. At a wavelength of 365 nm, the optimized hybrid device exhibits a photoresponsivity of approximately 0.012 A/W and a gain of 328. Furthermore, under a bias of 0.01 V, the maximum photocurrent-to-dark current ratio at 365 nm reaches ∼6200, representing an improvement by nearly sixfold over the standard reference device.

All chemicals, obtained from Aladdin, and Beijing Chemical Reagent (Beijing, China), were used without further purification. All standard customized Si photodetectors (SGP416), purchased without a transparent glass window as a protective layer, are identical in their basic characteristics and have not been further improved. Deionized water (resistivity ∼14 MΩ cm−1) was utilized in all experiments to ensure the elimination of impurities. Solid fnCQDs were subjected to vacuum drying at 65 °C for 20 h prior to characterization, ensuring the removal of any residual solvents and water content.

The synthesized fnQCDs was examined under varying magnifications using a TEM analyzer (Phillips/FEI CM12) equipped with Docu Version 3.2 image analysis software. The corresponding particle size distribution was determined by analyzing images of at least 60 particles obtained from TEM observations. FTIR spectra of fnQCDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, IRAffinity, Shimadzu). The fluorescence and phosphorescence characteristics were analyzed with a PerkinElmer (LS 50B) fluorescence spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained using a Kratos Analytical (Shimadzu) spectrometer. The absorption property of the water-soluble fnCQDs solutions was measured in air by UV–vis/near-IR spectrometer (Lambda 950, PerkinElmer). For photoresponsivity measurements, monochromatic light (300–1100 nm) produced by a tungsten halogen lamp coupled with a monochromator (Newport CS260-RG-2-FH-D) was used, modulated at 16 Hz via a chopper. Calibrated Ge and Si photodetectors (NIST standards) served to calibrate the light power at various wavelengths. The incident light was focused within the photodetector's active area. Short-circuit current measurements were performed using a Keithley 2400 power source under 0 V bias. Time- and power-dependent photoresponses were analyzed at 365 nm. All measurements were conducted in ambient conditions at room temperature.

fnCQDs was synthesized by using single-step functionalizing method [19]. To synthesize fnCQDs, 2 g of 2,4-difluorobenzoic acid and 2.4 g of glycine were combined in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave with 40 ml of deionized water (20 MΩ cm−1). The mixture was heated at 220 °C for 40 h under static conditions. After the mixed solution was cooled down to room temperature (20 °C), the resulting brown solution was purified by filtering through a dialysis bag against deionized water to remove any impurities. As a result of the synthesis process, a solution containing functionalized carbon quantum dots (fnCQDs) was successfully obtained.

To refine the fnCQDs layer, solutions with concentrations of 2 mg mL−1, 3 mg mL−1, 4 mg mL−1, and 5 mg mL−1 were systematically prepared for the devices designated as fnD2, fnD3, fnD4, and fnD5, respectively. The fabrication of the Si photodetector integrated with fnCQDs was achieved using a straightforward one-step drop-coating method. Initially, the standard Si photodetector was irradiated with ultraviolet light for 5 min to enhance surface activation. Subsequently, 10 μL of 99 % ethanol was drop-coated onto the surface and allowed to evaporate and dry naturally, improving the hydrophilicity of the device surface. Following this pre-treatment, the fnCQDs solution, prepared at various concentrations as described earlier, was drop-coated onto the surface of the photodetector. The coated device was then annealed in a vacuum furnace at 70 °C for 15 min to remove residual moisture, resulting in the formation of a uniform fnCQDs layer on the silicon photodetector, thereby completing the fabrication of the fnCQDs hybrid Si photodetector.

To investigate the surface morphology of fnCQDs, TEM and HRTEM analyses were performed. As shown in Fig. 1 a–c, the fnCQDs exhibit well-ordered monodispersity with an average particle size of 6 nm and distinct lattice fringe spacing of 0.2 nm, corresponding precisely to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [28,29]. To probe the structural information of the fnCQDs, the XPS characterization was performed. As portrayed in Fig. 1d the wide-scan XPS spectra clearly reveals that the carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and fluorine peaks located at 284.7, 400.2, 533.8, and 687.4 eV, respectively and the elemental composition analysis of fnCQDs revealed carbon (61.3 %), oxygen (19.2 %), nitrogen (14.3 %), and fluorine (5.2 %). To better understand the chemical bonding composition of the sample, high-resolution XPS measurement was carried out. The C 1s spectra of fnCQDs show the peaks at 284.7 eV corresponding to sp3 hybridized carbons (Fig. 1e), whereas the peaks 286.2 eV, 286.9 eV, and 288.3 eV are attributed to C–O/C–N, CO and C–F bond, respectively [30], indicating nitrogen and fluorine are successfully functionalized on CQDs. In the F 1s spectra (Fig. 1f), the C–F bond, comprising a covalent C–F bond (687.8 eV) and a semi-ionic C–F bond (685.8 eV), was observed exclusively in the fnCQDs [25]. Moreover, the N 1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 399.5 eV (pyrrolic N) and 401.5 eV (graphitic N), corresponding to N–H and C–N bonds, respectively [30], which confirms the significant nitrogen doping levels.

To further explore the properties of fnCQDs, the Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), the UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed. As revealed in Fig. 2 a the prominent peaks associated with oxygen-containing functional group, including semi-ionic C–F bonds (1116 cm−1), covalent C–F bonds (1216 cm−1) and C–N bending modes (1416 cm−1), are clearly observed in the fnCQDs [31,32]. Meanwhile, the three peaks at 1626 cm−1, 3289 cm−1 and 3449 cm−1 can be assigned to CO stretching in the amide bond, the stretching vibration of –NH2, and the stretching vibration of –OH, respectively [31]. The analysis confirms the presence of functional groups, including C–F, –OH, –CONH2, and –COOH, in the synthesized fnCQDs. Notably, the –OH and –COOH groups significantly enhance their water solubility. As illustrated in Fig. 2b, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum reveals a pronounced absorption capability in the short-wavelength region, particularly below 520 nm, suggesting the potential of the fnCQDs for applications in short-wavelength photodetection. Furthermore, the fnCQDs exhibit a maximum photoluminescent emission peak at 513 (PLQY = 57 %) when excited at 466 nm, demonstrating their efficient light emission properties. The bandgap energy of the fnCQDs, calculated to be 2.54 eV (Fig. 2c), is consistent with the observed photophysical characteristics, confirming that the emission originates from the band-edge exciton-state decay [33,34]. The photoluminescence spectra of the fnCQDs, recorded at excitation wavelengths ranging from 250 nm to 550 nm, exhibit emission peaks within the range of 470–570 nm. These emissions can be attributed to a combination of defect-state luminescence and bandgap luminescence, reflecting the contributions of both surface defects and intrinsic electronic transitions [35]. Notably, the emission peak aligns with the high-response wavelength range of conventional Si photodetectors, indicating that the synthesized fnCQDs can effectively convert high-energy incident photons into lower-energy emitted photons through fluorescence mediated by defect-state luminescence. This mechanism offers a promising approach to enhancing the short-wavelength sensitivity of Si photodetectors.

Fig. 3 a presents a schematic of the hybrid Si photodetector structure, which incorporates an ultrathin layer of fnCQDs through a simple, cost-effective, one-step solution drop-coating method as we previously developed (for further details, see the Experimental section) [24]. The film formation of fnCQDs at varying concentrations on the Si photodetector surface is expected to influence the device performance. To optimize the hybrid Si photodetector design, four different fnCQD solution concentrations (2 mg ml−1, 3 mg ml−1, 4 mg ml−1, and 5 mg ml−1) were applied to the surface of commercial Si photodetectors, resulting in four distinct device configurations, hereafter referred to as fnD2, fnD3, fnD4, and fnD5, respectively. Compared to commercial standard reference devices, the as-prepared hybrid photodetector demonstrated a pronounced bright green fluorescence when exposed to incident light at 365 nm (Fig. 3b). This visible fluorescence, readily discernible to the naked eye, corresponds to the fnCQDs emission band centered at 513 nm, as depicted in Fig. 2c. The observed fluorescence validates the occurrence of an efficient photoluminescence downconversion process, wherein high-energy ultraviolet photons are effectively converted into lower-energy visible light. This phenomenon underscores the functional integration of the fnCQDs layer within the hybrid device, highlighting its potential for enhanced optoelectronic performance. To further investigate the morphology of fnCQDs deposited on the Si photodetector surface, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) analyses were performed. Fig. 3c and d reveal a uniformly distributed layer of solution-processed fnCQDs across a large area, predominantly comprising zero to two monolayers with occasional empty regions. Notably, no significant granular agglomeration was observed, indicating a controlled deposition process. This relatively homogeneous and ultrathin functional layer is pivotal for facilitating the efficient downconversion of short-wavelength photons into longer wavelengths, thereby improving their absorption and utilization by the silicon substrate. The uniformity and minimal aggregation of fnCQDs are essential for ensuring consistent optical performance and enhancing device efficiency.

To investigate the impact of incorporating an fnCQDs functional layer on the photoelectric performance of Si photodetectors, the photoresponse characteristics of the fabricated devices were evaluated across a wavelength range of 300–1100 nm. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, all hybrid devices exhibited significantly enhanced photoresponsivity in the short-wavelength range (300–550 nm). Among them, the fnD4 device demonstrated the most pronounced improvement, while the fnD5 device showed the least enhancement. This observation suggests that the performance enhancement of standard Si photodetectors by fnCQDs is not linearly correlated with the concentration of the fnCQDs solution but instead exhibits a trade-off. This phenomenon may be attributed to the increased thickness of the fnCQDs functional layer with higher solution concentrations, which affects the total amount of incident light absorbed by the Si photodetector. Notably, the inclusion of the additional fnCQDs film had minimal influence on the absorptance of the silicon photodetector in the longer wavelength range (700–1100 nm). As a result, the photodetection capabilities beyond 700 nm remained virtually unchanged, preserving the device's sensitivity in this region. This characteristic ensures that the hybrid device maintains consistent broadband responsivity across the UV–Vis–NIR spectrum, illustrated Fig. 4b. The ability to enhance short-wavelength performance while retaining near-infrared detection capabilities highlights the potential of fnCQDs functional layers for advanced, broadband photodetection applications, providing a versatile solution for various optoelectronic system. Moreover, in the short-wavelength range of 300–550 nm, the fnD4 device exhibited a 56 % improvement in optical response at 365 nm compared to the standard reference device, outperforming the fnD2 and fnD5 devices by 19 % and 28 %, respectively (Fig. 4c). These experimental results demonstrate that the incorporation of an additional fluorescent fnCQDs layer significantly enhances the responsivity and sensitivity of silicon photodiodes in the short-wavelength region. Notably, the observed enhancement surpasses that achieved with conventional pure CQDs by more than a factor of one [24], indicating that nitrogen and fluorine doping substantially improves the photoelectric properties of CQDs. The enhanced short-wavelength range responsivity can be attributed to two primary mechanisms (Fig. 4d). The first mechanism is radiative energy transfer (RET). When fnCQDs absorb shorter-

wavelength light (e.g., ultraviolet or blue light), they subsequently emit longer-wavelength light (e.g., green light around 513 nm). This fluorescence emission, resulting from spectral downshifting, overlaps with the high absorption band of Si. Consequently, energy is transferred to the active region of the Si photodetector via radiative transitions, thereby enhancing the photoresponse of the detector [36–39]. The second is nonradiative energy transfer (NRET). Here, fnCQDs transfer energy to the surface of the adjacent Si photodetector through nonradiative processes. The excitation energy of the quantum dots is directly transferred to carriers on the p-Si surface or within the p-Si material via near-field interactions (e.g., electric dipole-electric dipole interactions), thereby improving the photoresponse of the photodetector [40–44].

The time- and power-dependent photoresponse of the fnD4 sample at room temperature was systematically investigated under monochromatic illumination (365 nm). As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the optimized hybrid device demonstrates an impressive photocurrent (IP) to dark current (ID) ratio, reaching approximately 6200 at a bias voltage of 0.01 V. This substantial ratio indicates that the photocurrent significantly exceeds the dark current by more than two orders of magnitude, which not only reflects a desirable reduction in current leakage but also highlights the device's exceptional signal-to-noise ratio. Such characteristics are indicative of the device's potential for high-performance photodetection applications [45,46]. Significantly, the fnD4 sample not only demonstrates consistent optical switching reliability under various incident laser powers (@365 nm) comparable to standard devices, but also achieves a remarkable photocurrent of 45 nA under 16 μW (Fig. 5b), highlighting its excellent short-wavelength photodetection capabilities. Besides, the photocurrent vs. wavelength and the corresponding gain (@ V = 0 V) of the fnD4 device were measured and shown in Fig. 5c. It can be clearly seen that the photocurrent of the fnD4 device is significantly higher than that of the standard device in the wavelength range of 300–450 nm, while the corresponding gain of the fnD4 device also increases steadily with wavelength and reaches a maximum peak of 328 (@365 nm). This demonstrates that the fnD4 sample has excellent short-wavelength photodetection performance, with enhanced photocurrent and gain, especially under UV irradiation. To further assess the performance of the developed hybrid device, the dynamic response under 365 nm light illumination with a power of 14 μW was measured, as shown in Fig. 5d. It can be observed that, over a 30-s long-term measurement, no significant reduction in photoresponse is observed, indicating the stable and reliable operational capability of the proposed hybrid device [47,48]. Additionally, the detailed analysis of the rising edges of the time-dependent photoresponse is shown in Fig. 5e. When the laser is turned on, large and rapid rising peaks are observed in both the standard and fnD4 devices, with the fnD4 device exhibiting a relatively longer rise time of 61 μs, compared to the reference blank Si device (47μs). This increase is attributed to the optical coupling between the fnCQDs and the underlying Si device, as well as the fluorescence lifetime of the fnCQDs film. The extended response time is likely due to the fluorescence lifetime of the incorporated fnCQD nanoparticles and their interaction with the underlying Si photodetector [49,50]. Overall, the photodetection capabilities of the proposed hybrid Si photodetector are promising, suggesting its potential for practical applications in high-performance, cost-effective, and reliable optoelectronic systems, as well as other functional applications.

In summary, we prepared water-soluble fnQCDs using a single-step functionalizing method and further investigated the possibility of fnCQDs as an additional functional layer of Si detectors to enhance the short-wavelength responsivity of Si photodetectors. Systematic investigations demonstrate that the fabricated hybrid device exhibits a broadband photoresponse, primarily attributed to the unique optoelectronic properties of the fnCQDs layer. This layer efficiently converts incident short-wavelength light into long-wavelength light, which aligns with the high-response region of the PIN Si photodetector. Consequently, the converted light is more effectively captured, significantly enhancing the photoresponse in the short-wavelength range. The optimized integration of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (fnCQDs) significantly enhances the responsivity of the device in the short-wavelength range while preserving the stable and reliable operational capability, comparable to those of the conventional bare Si device. The optimized hybrid device exhibits a photoresponsivity of approximately 0.012 A/W and a gain of 328 (@365 nm). Furthermore, under a bias of 0.01 V, the maximum photocurrent-to-dark current ratio at 365 nm reaches ∼6200, representing an improvement by nearly sixfold over the standard reference device. The proposed approach, characterized by its simple architecture and cost-effectiveness, ensures a promising photodetector technology compatible with Si process technology through a convenient fabrication scheme.

Zhou Huang: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Feng Nan: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Zhilong Zhang: Formal analysis, Data curation. Weiyu Feng: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Lei Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (Grant No. 24KJA140002), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61775076), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Grant Nos. BK20201476, BK20201067).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. W. Ing, G. C. Gerhard, A high gain silicon photodetector, Proc. IEEE. 53 (1965) 1714-1722.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Casalino, G. Coppola, R. M. De La Rue, D. F. Logan, State-of-the-art all-silicon sub-bandgap photodetectors at telecom and datacom wavelengths, Laser Photonics Rev. 10 (2016) 895-921.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress of silicon integrated light emitters and photodetectors for optical communication based on two-dimensional materials

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integrated germanium optical interconnects on silicon substrates

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silicon carbide ultraviolet photodetectors and their application in ecological monitoring

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silicon‐compatible photodetectors: trends to monolithically integrate photosensors with chip technology

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional nanocrystalline TiO2 thin films for UV enhanced highly responsive silicon photodetectors

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High quantum efficiency amorphous silicon photodetectors with picosecond response times
